summary: This notebook implements an optimized inference pipeline to generate competition submissions for locating bacterial flagellar motors in 3D tomograms. It loads pre-trained YOLOv8 weights, processes test slices with GPU-accelerated parallelization, and applies 3D non-maximum suppression to consolidate detections across slices. The author demonstrates how to efficiently handle large-scale medical imaging inference by dynamically adjusting batch sizes, utilizing half-precision computation, and overlapping I/O with GPU processing. Readers should learn practical techniques for deploying object detection models in resource-constrained or time-sensitive Kaggle environments.
competition_flow: Loads pre-trained YOLOv8 weights, runs optimized GPU inference on test tomogram slices, applies 3D NMS to merge detections, and exports the top prediction per tomogram to a CSV submission file.
data_reading: Reads test data as directories of .jpg slice files per tomogram, loading them via cv2.imread with PIL as a fallback.
data_processing: Normalizes slice intensities using 2nd and 98th percentile clipping.
Subsamples slices across the z-axis using a configurable CONCENTRATION ratio to control processing speed.
ensembling: Applies 3D non-maximum suppression to cluster and merge nearby detections across slices, then selects the single highest-confidence detection per tomogram for the final submission.
insights: Dynamic batch sizing based on available GPU memory prevents out-of-memory errors while maximizing throughput.
Fusing convolutional and batch normalization layers and enabling FP16 inference significantly accelerates YOLOv8 prediction.
Overlapping image preloading with GPU inference via threading reduces I/O bottlenecks during large-scale processing.
3D NMS effectively consolidates redundant slice-level detections into coherent 3D motor coordinates.
critical_findings: Subsampling slices via the CONCENTRATION parameter trades detection recall for speed, making it useful for pipeline verification rather than final scoring.
GPU memory availability directly dictates the maximum feasible batch size, requiring runtime calculation rather than static configuration.
